lines.text: The hallway smells like new paint and old pizza. Lockers slam like punctuation on conversations she's not part of.
Okay so. New school. Totally normal. I've done this before.
(I have never done this before.)

lines.action: scans locker numbers, mouthing them slightly

lines.text: Everyone already knows where they're going. Everyone already knows everyone. I could just turn around. Fake a fever. Very convincing fever.
A group of girls laughs near the trophy case. It's not about her. It feels like it's about her.
Locker 214. Don't do this to me.
Fine. I'm fine. Find the locker. Go.
Tyler leans against a locker, watching her walk like he's been waiting for exactly this.
Yo, new kid alert — bet she brought a name tag and everything.
I mean — okay that was kind of funny —
lines.action: laughs half a beat late, glances at Tyler

lines.text: Tour costs five bucks. Cash only. Crying's extra.
Heart's doing something dumb. Don't let it show. Don't—
...Well? You gonna say something or just stand there?
Down the hall, a girl with an easy grin peels off from her friends.
Okay so — bold move, introducing yourself like that on day one. I respect it.
Honestly? I rehearsed that in my head like four times before I said it out loud.
Yeah, I could tell. In a good way.
I'm Priya. Come find me at lunch if you want.
An open door. Weird. Didn't know I needed one until right now.
She keeps her head down, mapping the terrain instead — who sits where, which hallway backs up like traffic right before the bell.
Okay so. Table by the window — clearly someone's spot. Noted. Don't sit there.
In class, near the back, a kid holds a battered field guide to birds up like a shield against the noise.
Huh. Bird person. Whatever — respect the commitment.
The bell rings. Before the sound even finishes, that kid is already gone — first one out, like they've done this a hundred times.
Filed that away for later. Not that there's a 'later.' Probably.
The cafeteria hits like a wall of sound — trays clattering, a hundred conversations already in progress without her.
Hey! Maya! Over here, if you want!
Yeah, come be our celebrity, new kid.
Jordan. Alone. Book up like armor. And there's an empty table by the windows, nobody's claimed it.
Okay so. Decision time. No pressure. (Massive pressure.)
Okay so — for real, that entrance? A-plus. Would watch again.
I peaked early. It's all downhill from here.
Don't undersell yourself, new kid.
So where'd you transfer from? Or is that, like, classified.
Somewhere with worse hallways, honestly.
I mean — that's fair —
Tyler's eyes drift across the cafeteria and land on the field guide poking out of Jordan's bag.
Careful — don't let it migrate on you.
The table laughs. Chloe half a beat behind, like always.

lines.action: doesn't laugh, taps the table twice, watches

lines.text: Every eye drifts to Maya. Waiting.
She's three bites into lunch when a shadow falls over the table.
Is the seat taken? — sorry, that's a stupid question, obviously it's a table, tables have seats, I just meant — yeah. Is it okay if I sit here.
lines.action: holds a book slightly raised, like a shield

lines.text: Jordan glances up, surprised, when Maya sits down across from them, book lowering half an inch.
Oh. Hi. You can — I mean, yeah, you can sit there. It's not, like, reserved.
Cool. I'm Maya. New, obviously.
Obviously. Three schools in four years — I've gotten really good at spotting the new-kid walk.
There's a walk?
There's a walk.
Across the cafeteria, Priya catches Maya's eye and waves — easy, unbothered, like it's no big deal either way.
You can go. It's fine. I'm not gonna, like, keep score.
lines.action: already retreating a little, book rising again

lines.text: ...I mean I might. But not out loud.
Lunch goes on. Easy. Loud. Exactly the kind of table she pictured sitting at.
See, she gets it.

lines.emotion: laughs, relieved someone else went first

lines.action: pokes at her tray, doesn't meet Maya's eyes

lines.text: This is what fitting in feels like, I guess. Kind of thought it'd feel better than this.
The rest of lunch is fine. Just a half-step stiffer than before.
...Yeah. Okay. Cool.
...Whatever, man. It's not that deep.

lines.action: picks at her bracelet, says nothing

lines.text: Didn't feel like a big moment. Felt like a small, uncomfortable one. Guess those count too.
Days pass. Priya's friendly enough — a wave at the lockers, a 'hey' by the water fountain.
Hey. Good luck on the quiz.
She doesn't ask her back to the table again. It isn't punishment. It's quieter than that.
Guess that's just how it goes sometimes. Nobody said anything. Nobody had to.
The next morning, Priya falls into step beside her at the locker.
Hey — that was a good thing you did.
lines.emotion: casual, like it's no big deal

lines.text: It was kind of a small thing.
Small things count.
I used to let stuff like that go all the time. Told myself it wasn't my place.
She doesn't finish the thought. It doesn't feel entirely about Tyler.
Nice, being seen like this. And noticing — that's not why I said it in the first place.
So. First day. How's it going so far, on a scale of 'fine' to 'actively considering the fire alarm.'
lines.action: settling in, still a little guarded

lines.text: Strong 'fire alarm' energy, honestly.
Same. I got lost twice looking for the library. It's right next to the front doors.
Okay, that's actually impressive.
Don't tell anyone. I have a reputation to protect. A reputation of having no reputation. It's very fragile.
Something in the tension between them loosens. Just a little.
I'm Jordan, by the way.
Maya.
Okay that was cold. Fix it. Say something.
...You don't have to talk to me. I get it.
No, I didn't mean—
It's fine. I've got a thing anyway.
Jordan's gone before the sentence even finishes. The seat across from her is empty again.
That didn't feel like nothing. Should've said something better.
So you're really not going over there.
Nah. You seem more interesting than whatever they're doing over there anyway.
Careful. That's a low bar.
So — three schools in four years?
Yeah. My mom's job. We move around a lot.
I've gotten really good at figuring out the fastest route to the bathroom and really bad at, uh, everything else about starting over.
That sounds exhausting.
It's fine. I'm used to it.
I don't know if I'll ever, like — actually feel settled anywhere. But it's fine.
Well. You're settled at this table, at least. For today.
...Yeah. Okay.
Priya's table absorbs her like she's always been there — easy, loud, laughing before she's even fully sat down.
Okay so — glad you came over. Sit, sit.
Took you long enough.
Across the cafeteria, Jordan sits alone again, book back up.
It's fine. They said it was fine. It's fine.
Jordan glances over, just once, then looks back down at the page.
Okay. Noted. Moving on.
Days later, in the library before homeroom, their eyes catch for half a second over the top of the field guide.
Jordan looks away first.
There it is. That thing that's not quite guilt. Worse, actually — it's knowing exactly which choice I made.
By fifth period, word's traveled the halls the way it does in a school this size. Maya barely notices — still thinking about lunch.
Partners for the semester project are on the board. No, I won't be switching them.
lines.action: taps two fingers on the desk

lines.text: Names go up: Jordan Kessler — Maya Ortiz-Chen.
Jordan's face does something complicated — surprise, then something like relief — before settling into a small, real smile.
Guess you're stuck with me.
Guess so. Try to contain your excitement.
...No promises.
By fifth period, the day's social math has mostly settled. Maya's part in it barely made a ripple.
Partners are on the board.
lines.action: pushes glasses up, though they haven't slipped

lines.text: Jordan Kessler — Maya Ortiz-Chen.
Jordan turns around — the girl with the field guide, the one Maya noticed and never quite met.
Hey. I'm Jordan. I think we're... a thing now. Project-wise.
Guess we are.
Cool. Cool cool cool.
By fifth period, a few extra glances follow Maya through the halls — curious, or knowing, depending on who saw what at lunch.
Partners are on the board.
Jordan Kessler — Maya Ortiz-Chen.
Jordan's expression barely changes. Already braced.
Guess we're partners.
lines.emotion: flat, not quite looking at her

lines.text: Fair. Exactly what fairness would predict. Somehow that stings more.
I've got most weekends free. Whatever works.
The library after school is quiet in a different way than the cafeteria — quiet like it's holding its breath.
Okay so, I was thinking we split it by section, but also — we could just talk through it together? Might be faster.
Together works.
Faster, probably. Also less likely I do it wrong alone.
You don't seem like a 'do it wrong' person.
I contain multitudes.
They work for a while. The silence between them stops being awkward and starts being just... silence.
This is my grandma's field guide, actually. The one you probably saw at lunch.
Yeah, I noticed. You held onto it like it mattered.
She died before I was old enough to remember her voice. So I just—
lines.action: trails off, sketches a shape in the air, a wing

lines.text: I don't know. Birds feel like the closest thing I've got.
Maya doesn't ask her to explain further. Somehow, that's exactly right.
The work session is efficient. Pages turn. Pencils move. Nobody says anything that isn't strictly necessary.
I can take the second half if you want to start the intro.
Fine.
Twenty minutes pass like that. Productive. Airtight. Nothing leaking through.
As they pack up, Jordan lingers, zipping her bag slower than she needs to.
...Anyway.
Nobody really asks that. Here, I mean. Or anywhere, lately.
I get it. Kind of. New places make you feel like a rerun nobody's watched before.
That's... weirdly accurate.
The field guide sits between them on the table. Neither of them mentions it. It doesn't need mentioning.
This was — this was nice. Today.
lines.action: searches for a cooler word, doesn't find one, says it anyway

lines.text: Yeah. It was.

lines.action: waits, a little too long, for something more

lines.text: Anyway — you want the intro or should I take it? Very important business decisions.
...I'll take it.
The moment passes. Pleasant. Closed like a book.
Wonder what would've happened if I'd just said the real thing.
Hey — you okay? You've got a face.
I don't know. Today just felt like... nobody really saw me. Until, I guess. Now.
That's a real thing. I've felt that. More than once, actually.
...Yeah?
Yeah.
Thanks. For asking. Most people don't.
It's a late start. But a real one.
Okay, see you tomorrow!
...Yeah. See you.
The bag zips shut. The chair scrapes back. The project's done — technically, a success.
That was fine. Everything's fine.
Why does 'fine' keep feeling like the wrong word.

options.text: Hi, I'm Maya — new, obviously. Where's the bathroom, actually?
Just keep walking. Head down.

b.text: Nope. Not today. Not ever, actually.
She puts her head down and keeps walking, ears burning.

options.text: I'll go sit with Priya.
I'll just sit here by myself. It's fine.
I'll go sit with Jordan.

a.text: Told you, five bucks well spent.

b.text: She sits at the empty table alone, unwrapping her lunch like it's the most interesting thing in the world.

c.text: She walks straight over to Jordan's table. Jordan looks up, surprised, and closes the field guide halfway.

options.text: Ha — yeah, okay, that's kind of funny.
That's not cool, honestly.

a.action: says nothing, looks at her fries

a.text: Victory. Feels weird in my mouth. Like biting into something and finding out it's hollow.

b.text: Relax, it's just a joke, God.
The table goes quiet a beat too long.

b.action: no laugh this time, looks between Tyler and Maya

b.text: Okay. That happened. No taking it back now.

options.text: Yeah, of course — sit.
I guess. Whatever.

b.action: face falls slightly, sits anyway, stiffer

options.text: Nah, I'm good here.
Actually — I'll go say hi.
Want to meet up after school? I feel like we could actually dig into this.
Let's just split it fair and easy — no pressure.
Want to meet after school and actually get to know each other a bit?
Let's just keep it simple — divide it up and go.
Hey — about lunch. That wasn't cool, whatever table I ended up at. Want to start over? Maybe meet after school?
So — should we just split the work and go from there?
Yeah, actually — I feel like I don't fit in anywhere yet either.
Eh, I mean, everybody's weird about something, right?

a.text: I've moved before too. Not this many times, but — enough to know that feeling.
Huh. Okay. That actually helps, weirdly.

options.text: Hey — you okay? You've got a face.
Okay, see you tomorrow!

a.text: I don't know. Today just felt like— nobody really saw me. Until, I guess. Now.

voiceNotes: Maya (player) thinks in run-on internal monologue that she never fully says out loud — the gap between INTERNAL lines and spoken lines IS her character. She rehearses, then either overshoots (fast, sarcastic) or clams up. Tyler's volume and nicknames are armor — 'new kid,' 'the migrate joke' — he needs the room's reaction to know he landed. When challenged, he deflates fast but won't admit it ('whatever, man'). Chloe's laugh is always a half-beat late because she's checking Tyler's face first — her silence after choice-3b is the loudest thing she does all episode. Priya doesn't lecture; she withdraws warmth quietly (fade) or extends it quietly (respect) — she never makes a speech about it, which is the point. Jordan's dialogue is built on self-interruption and over-qualifying ('sorry that's a stupid question') until they're safe, at which point sentences get longer, more specific, more alive (the bird tangent). Ms. Alvarez should feel almost bored by her own power — she's already decided everything before she says a word; the two-finger tap signals 'this is final,' not 'let's discuss.'

alternatives: Fresh meat! Tour costs five bucks — cash only, no refunds.
Oh look, a new one. Somebody get the welcome package.
alternativeLines.reasoning: Original leans into 'name tag' as a specific, image-based jab rather than generic — feels more like something Tyler's used before and will use again, per his 'label sticks' pattern.

alternatives: I guess birds are just... the closest thing I've got left of her.
It's dumb, probably. But birds feel like the closest thing I've got.
alternativeLines.reasoning: Kept the line close to the character profile's exact wording since it's a signature reveal line — minor variants offered in case 'It's dumb, probably' reads as too self-deprecating for this version of Jordan post-connection.

alternatives: Why does 'fine' feel like a lie I told myself on purpose.
alternativeLines.reasoning: Original keeps Maya's internal voice self-aware but unresolved — matches her pattern of catching her own deflection without necessarily fixing it.
